One full day. Alcohol typically clears from the body within this window. For many people, tonight's sleep — though sometimes restless — feels different from the nights that came before.

Most alcohol metabolites have left your body. The brain's GABA system is recalibrating — this can bring restlessness, but it means your nervous system is finding its natural balance again. Hydration is improving.
One full week. Many people start to notice sharper thinking, more natural energy, and better hydration around this stage. Your body has had a meaningful stretch of recovery time.
Two weeks. For many people, anxiety begins to stabilise and sleep deepens. The early-recovery storm often starts to soften here, though every person's timeline is different.
One month. Many people describe meaningful gains in clarity and emotional steadiness at this point. Cravings can become easier to observe without acting on them.

Two months. Research suggests the prefrontal cortex — responsible for decisions, impulse control, and empathy — begins to recover meaningfully around this stage. Many people see improvements in cholesterol levels. You are physically and neurologically different from who you were.
Three months. Skin can look clearer, sleep can feel deeper, and concentration often continues to sharpen. You have built real momentum.
Six months. Many people report that around this point, sobriety has begun to feel like part of who they are — not just a goal they're chasing.

One year. This is a profound milestone. Many people describe genuine, lasting changes in how they feel and how they relate to themselves. The cumulative gains of a year without alcohol are real — and they are yours.
How your mind and body are restoring themselves
{days, plural, =1{1 day sober} other{{days} days sober}}
Your body begins adjusting to the absence of alcohol. Hydration, sleep pressure, blood sugar, and stress hormones may feel unsettled as your system begins to rebalance.
For many people, the body's basic rhythms — heart rate, hydration, sleep — start to shift as it adjusts. This can feel calming for some and uncomfortable for others.
For people who were drinking heavily, this can be one of the highest-risk windows for withdrawal symptoms. Your nervous system may feel overstimulated as it works to rebalance.
For many people, the most intense early physical adjustment begins to ease around this point, though recovery is individual and some symptoms can continue.
Restorative sleep often begins to return. Hydration, appetite, and daily energy may start to feel more stable, although sleep and mood can still fluctuate.
Physical stamina may begin to return as sleep, appetite, hydration, and daily rhythm become more stable.
Your body has had a meaningful stretch of relief from the strain of alcohol. Many people notice steadier energy, clearer thinking, and improved sleep around this stage.
Your body may feel more resilient as sleep, nourishment, movement, and reduced alcohol strain begin working together.
Many people notice a steadier baseline by this stage. Stress may feel more manageable, sleep may feel more reliable.
For many people, the long-term load on energy, sleep, and mood is meaningfully lighter after a year without alcohol.
The benefits of reduced alcohol strain can continue to deepen over time, supporting your body, mind, relationships, and daily sense of stability.

Encrypted backups (.jfwbk) require your passphrase to open. Plain JSON backups are unencrypted — store them somewhere only you can access. Vision-board photos themselves aren't bundled into the backup file (only the references); if you restore on a new device, you may need to re-attach those images.

Journey Forward stores everything on your device only. No data is ever sent to any server.
All data stays on your device

Every piece of information you enter — your sober date, journal entries, gratitude notes, slip records, and profile — is stored locally on your device using the operating system's standard app storage. Nothing is transmitted to any external server, cloud service, or third party at any time.

Journey Forward works fully offline. All fonts and assets are bundled inside the app. The app itself makes no network requests. If you tap a link to a crisis line, support group, or external resource, your device will open it in your system browser — outside of the app and subject to that site's own privacy policy.
There are no analytics SDKs, crash reporters, or usage trackers in this app. We do not collect any data about how you use the app, how often you open it, or what features you use.
If you add an emergency contact, their name and phone number are stored only on your device as part of your profile. This information is never shared, synced, or backed up automatically.

When you export a backup, a file is created and shared via your device's share sheet — the same way you share photos. You can choose an encrypted backup (.jfwbk, protected by your passphrase) or a plain JSON file. Journey Forward does not receive or store this file. You control where it goes.

If you enable a PIN, it is salted and run through a slow key-derivation hash (PBKDF2-style), then stored in your device's encrypted storage — never as plaintext. Biometric unlock uses your device's native biometric system; Journey Forward never accesses or stores your biometric data.

To permanently delete all your data, simply uninstall the app. All data stored by Journey Forward is removed when the app is uninstalled. There is no account to delete because there is no account — only data on your device.
Journey Forward is designed for adults aged 18 and over. The app is not directed at children and does not knowingly collect any information from anyone under the age of 18.
If this privacy policy changes, the update will be included in a new app version. Since we collect no data, changes will only reflect improvements in transparency or new features added to the app.

Context: Short healing-timeline body text on the home recovery banner for the 1-week milestone.

Context: Short healing-timeline body text on the home recovery banner for the 3-month milestone.

English: Many people notice a steadier baseline. Urges may become less frequent and easier to move through.
Context: Short healing-timeline body text on the home recovery banner for the 6-month milestone.

English: For many people, the long-term load on sleep, energy, and mood begins to ease at this point.
Context: Short healing-timeline body text on the home recovery banner for the 1-year milestone.

English: Journey Forward is not a medical device and does not diagnose, treat, cure, or prevent any medical condition. This timeline is educational and reflects general recovery patterns only. Individual recovery varies. If you drink heavily, have a history of withdrawal, seizures, hallucinations, confusion, or feel physically unsafe, speak with a healthcare professional before stopping suddenly or seek urgent medical care.
Context: Medical/safety disclaimer shown above the healing timeline on the Recovery screen.
Uppercase section label introducing the psychological/mind notes for a recovery milestone.

English: You might feel a mix of relief and anxiety as your daily routine shifts. This is the normal friction of change.
Context: Recovery milestone 1 (12 hours) — mind/psychological note.

English: The first urges may appear. They can feel urgent, but they are temporary waves.
Context: Recovery milestone 1 (12 hours) — what you may experience.

English: Drink a large glass of water. When an urge hits, focus only on getting through the next hour.
Context: Recovery milestone 1 (12 hours) — actionable tip.

English: Your brain's reward circuitry is noticing the absence of its usual chemical trigger, which can cause irritability or a low mood.
Context: Recovery milestone 2 (24 hours) — mind/psychological note.

English: You may feel emotionally raw, tired, or slightly restless.
Context: Recovery milestone 2 (24 hours) — what you may experience.

English: Sleep and rest are your best allies right now. Keep your evening routine calm, quiet, and consistent.
Context: Recovery milestone 2 (24 hours) — actionable tip.

English: Your system is seeking balance. The intensity you feel right now is the feeling of that adjustment taking place.
Context: Recovery milestone 3 (48 hours) — mind/psychological note.

English: Restlessness and strong urges are common here. You might feel “wired” or on edge.
Context: Recovery milestone 3 (48 hours) — what you may experience.

English: Be especially patient with yourself today. If you experience shaking, confusion, hallucinations, seizures, severe agitation, or feel unsafe, seek urgent medical support.
Context: Recovery milestone 3 (48 hours) — actionable tip with safety warning.

English: The mental fog often begins to thin. Neurotransmitter production starts to slowly adjust, paving the way for more natural energy.
Context: Recovery milestone 4 (3 days) — mind/psychological note.

English: A small window of calm may emerge. You might feel a quiet, cautious optimism taking root.
Context: Recovery milestone 4 (3 days) — what you may experience.

English: Reaching 72 hours is meaningful. Mark it with comfort, care, and support.
Context: Recovery milestone 4 (3 days) — actionable tip.

English: You may notice unusually vivid dreams — some people experience this as their sleep pattern settles into a new rhythm.
Context: Recovery milestone 5 (1 week) — mind/psychological note.

English: Improved clarity, though your mood may still naturally swing up and down.
Context: Recovery milestone 5 (1 week) — what you may experience.

English: Anchor yourself in routine. A predictable morning and evening structure is a powerful tool right now.
Context: Recovery milestone 5 (1 week) — actionable tip.

English: Concentration and short-term memory often start to feel sharper. Each healthier choice you repeat helps lay down new patterns.
Context: Recovery milestone 6 (2 weeks) — mind/psychological note.

English: You might start feeling surprisingly well, though random moments of emptiness are still normal.
Context: Recovery milestone 6 (2 weeks) — what you may experience.

English: This is when overconfidence can sneak in. Stay connected to your daily practices and support systems.
Context: Recovery milestone 6 (2 weeks) — actionable tip.

English: The brain systems involved in impulse control, decision-making, and emotional regulation may begin to feel steadier over time.
Context: Recovery milestone 7 (1 month) — mind/psychological note.

English: Emotional regulation continues to improve, and building resilience becomes a steady practice.
Context: Recovery milestone 7 (1 month) — what you may experience.

English: Review your journey so far. Note the situations that still feel tricky, and plan how you will navigate them gracefully.
Context: Recovery milestone 7 (1 month) — actionable tip.

English: For many people, the ability to find genuine satisfaction in simple, everyday activities slowly returns at this stage.
Context: Recovery milestone 8 (3 months) — mind/psychological note.

English: Many people describe feeling more like themselves again. Motivation may feel more available, though it can still rise and fall.
Context: Recovery milestone 8 (3 months) — what you may experience.

English: Continue to cultivate your environment. Hobbies, nature, and relationships are deeply protective elements of your growth.
Context: Recovery milestone 8 (3 months) — actionable tip.

English: Urges may become less frequent or easier to move through.
Context: Recovery milestone 9 (6 months) — mind/psychological note.

English: The highs and lows of early recovery begin to smooth out into a more consistent rhythm.
Context: Recovery milestone 9 (6 months) — what you may experience.

English: Take a moment to honor the quiet days. Peace and stability are among the quiet rewards of this process.
Context: Recovery milestone 9 (6 months) — actionable tip.

English: You have lived through many seasons, routines, and emotional moments with more awareness and care.
Context: Recovery milestone 10 (1 year) — mind/psychological note.

English: Support may still matter, and needing it does not diminish your progress.
Context: Recovery milestone 10 (1 year) — what you may experience.

English: Reflect on the person you were twelve months ago. Write them a letter from where you stand today.
Context: Recovery milestone 10 (1 year) — actionable tip.

English: Recovery may feel less like something you are forcing and more like a way of living you have grown into.
Context: Recovery milestone 11 (2 years & beyond) — mind/psychological note.

English: Your story may become a source of comfort for someone else. When the moment feels right, share your strength with someone just beginning their path.
Context: Recovery milestone 11 (2 years & beyond) — actionable tip.

English:
Recovery and personal growth are rarely a straight line. Having walked a difficult road myself, I know how heavy some days can feel — and how exhausting it can be to use tools filled with noise, pressure, and distraction.

When you are trying to heal or rebuild, the last thing you need is advertising, attention-grabbing notifications, or the worry that your deeply personal reflections are being harvested.

Your recovery is not a data product.

I built Journey Forward to be a quiet alternative: no ads, no accounts, no tracking analytics, and no built-in cloud sync. It is designed as a private, offline-first sanctuary for honest days and steady progress.

Because Journey Forward has no accounts, analytics, tracking, or cloud sync, I have no way of seeing how you experience the app, what feels confusing, or what features might help you most. If something is not working, or if you have an idea for a future improvement, you are welcome to contact me directly.

This app is not here to shame you, score you, or punish you for difficult moments. It is here to help you return — to your reason, your routines, your breath, and the next small step forward.

I am also working toward language support, including Zulu and Afrikaans, so Journey Forward can become more welcoming while keeping its privacy-first foundation.

My hope is that this space helps you find grounding, reflection, and the grace to take one honest step at a time.

— Shawn

Title of the Urge Surfing guided meditation.

English: Close your eyes and take three slow breaths.

English: Notice the craving. Where do you feel it in your body?

English: Imagine it as a wave in the ocean — rising slowly.

English: Now watch it begin to fall. Urges always fade.

English: You did not drink. The wave passed. You surfed it.

Title of the Body Scan guided meditation.

English: Lie down or sit comfortably. Close your eyes.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 1 of 7.

English: Bring attention to your feet. Notice any sensation — warmth, tingling.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 2 of 7. Keep the em dash.

English: Slowly move up to your calves, then knees, then thighs.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 3 of 7.

English: Notice your belly rising and falling with each breath.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 4 of 7.

English: Scan your chest, shoulders, arms, and hands.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 5 of 7.

English: Finally, relax your jaw, eyes, and forehead.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 6 of 7.

English: Rest here for a moment. You are safe. You are whole.
Context: Body Scan meditation step 7 of 7.

English: What did they do or say that mattered to you?

English: Quiet patterns from your own check-ins — kept on this device, no judgement.
As you log how cravings go and what you did about them, this page fills with what actually works for you. Nothing to get right — just keep checking in.
When you tried these, here's how often the urge passed without a slip.
{count} of your {total} logged cravings landed around {window}. Worth planning something steadying for then.

The situations you've named most often:
{count, plural, =1{1 urge ridden out} other{{count} urges ridden out}}
{count, plural, =1{stayed sober through 1 craving} other{stayed sober through {count} cravings}}
YOUR PLAN WHEN A CRAVING HITS
You haven't written a plan yet. A few lines now can carry you through a hard moment later.
Slips are information, not failure. Every line here is something you learned by showing up.
These four shift your body chemistry in minutes — no thinking required. Pick one and follow along.
Cold on your face slows a racing heart fast.
Fill a bowl with cold water, or grab a cold pack or ice.
Hold your breath and put your face in the cold water — or hold the cold to your eyes and cheeks — for about 30 seconds.
Notice your body settle as your heart rate drops. Repeat once if you need to.
A short burst burns off the surge of stress hormones.
Pick something you can do hard for a short burst — jumping jacks, running on the spot, fast stairs.
Go all-out for 1 to 5 minutes, until you're a little out of breath.
Let your breathing come back down. The urge usually drops with it.
Longer out-breaths than in-breaths switch on the body's calming system.
Follow the circle. The out-breath is the longest part.
Tense as you breathe in, release as you breathe out — tension leaves with the breath.
Breathe in and tense a muscle group — fists, shoulders, or jaw — firmly but not to the point of pain.
Hold the tension for a few seconds while you notice it.
Breathe out and let it go all at once. Move through your body, group by group.
Need more than this right now?
